# 1. Prepare the hyperparameter summary data
pop_summary <- fit_ml_wba$summary(
  variables = c("mu[1]", "mu[2]", "sigma[1]", "sigma[2]", "Omega[1,2]")
) |>
  mutate(
    true_value = c(pop_logit_rho_mean, pop_log_kappa_mean,
                   pop_logit_rho_sd, pop_log_kappa_sd, pop_correlation),
    # Clean labels for the plot
    clean_var = c("Mean: logit(ρ)", "Mean: log(κ)", 
                  "SD: logit(ρ)", "SD: log(κ)", "Correlation: Ω")
  )

# Extract point estimates for the distributions
pop_est <- pop_summary |> dplyr::select(variable, mean) |> deframe()

# 2. Transform unpooled MAP estimates to the unconstrained population scale
unpooled_pop <- unpooled_ests |>
  mutate(
    logit_rho = qlogis(pmin(pmax(unpooled_rho, 0.001), 0.999)),
    log_kappa = log(pmax(unpooled_kappa, 0.001)) # safeguard against exactly 0
  )

# 3. Calculate summary statistics of the unpooled estimates
unpooled_rho_mean <- mean(unpooled_pop$logit_rho)
unpooled_rho_sd   <- sd(unpooled_pop$logit_rho)
unpooled_kappa_mean <- mean(unpooled_pop$log_kappa)
unpooled_kappa_sd   <- sd(unpooled_pop$log_kappa)
unpooled_cor      <- cor(unpooled_pop$logit_rho, unpooled_pop$log_kappa)

# Create a dataframe of these unpooled sample statistics
unpooled_hyper <- tibble(
  clean_var = c("Mean: logit(ρ)", "Mean: log(κ)", 
                "SD: logit(ρ)", "SD: log(κ)", "Correlation: Ω"),
  unpooled_stat = c(unpooled_rho_mean, unpooled_kappa_mean, 
                    unpooled_rho_sd, unpooled_kappa_sd, unpooled_cor)
)

# Join with pop_summary for the forest plot
plot_hyper_data <- pop_summary |>
  left_join(unpooled_hyper, by = "clean_var") |>
  mutate(clean_var = fct_rev(factor(clean_var, levels = clean_var)))

# 4. Create grid points for ALL theoretical Normal distributions
x_rho   <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 200)
x_kappa <- seq(-1, 3, length.out = 200)

dist_rho <- tibble(
  x = x_rho,
  True = dnorm(x, pop_logit_rho_mean, pop_logit_rho_sd),
  Hierarchical = dnorm(x, pop_est["mu[1]"], pop_est["sigma[1]"]),
  Unpooled = dnorm(x, unpooled_rho_mean, unpooled_rho_sd)
) |> pivot_longer(-x, names_to = "Distribution", values_to = "density")

dist_kappa <- tibble(
  x = x_kappa,
  True = dnorm(x, pop_log_kappa_mean, pop_log_kappa_sd),
  Hierarchical = dnorm(x, pop_est["mu[2]"], pop_est["sigma[2]"]),
  Unpooled = dnorm(x, unpooled_kappa_mean, unpooled_kappa_sd)
) |> pivot_longer(-x, names_to = "Distribution", values_to = "density")

# 5. Build the plots
p_pop_rho <- ggplot() +
  geom_area(data = filter(dist_rho, Distribution == "Unpooled"), 
            aes(x = x, y = density, fill = "Unpooled Estimates"), alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(data = filter(dist_rho, Distribution != "Unpooled"), 
            aes(x = x, y = density, color = Distribution, linetype = Distribution), 
            linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("True" = "black", "Hierarchical" = "#4575b4")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("True" = "dashed", "Hierarchical" = "solid")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Unpooled Estimates" = "grey60")) +
  labs(title = "Population Distribution: logit(ρ)", 
       subtitle = "Notice how the unpooled estimates are artificially too wide.",
       x = "logit(ρ)", y = "Density", fill = NULL, color = NULL, linetype = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p_pop_kappa <- ggplot() +
  geom_area(data = filter(dist_kappa, Distribution == "Unpooled"), 
            aes(x = x, y = density, fill = "Unpooled Estimates"), alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(data = filter(dist_kappa, Distribution != "Unpooled"), 
            aes(x = x, y = density, color = Distribution, linetype = Distribution), 
            linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("True" = "black", "Hierarchical" = "#d73027")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("True" = "dashed", "Hierarchical" = "solid")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Unpooled Estimates" = "grey60")) +
  labs(title = "Population Distribution: log(κ)", 
       subtitle = "The hierarchical model recovers the true, tighter variance.",
       x = "log(κ)", y = "Density", fill = NULL, color = NULL, linetype = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

# Forest plot for exact hyperparameter recovery
p_hyper <- ggplot(plot_hyper_data, aes(y = clean_var)) +
  # Hierarchical Posterior (Line and Dot)
  geom_pointrange(aes(x = mean, xmin = q5, xmax = q95, color = "Hierarchical"), 
                  size = 0.8, linewidth = 1) +
  # Unpooled Sample Statistic (Diamond)
  geom_point(aes(x = unpooled_stat, color = "Unpooled"), 
             shape = 18, size = 5) +
  # True Generating Value (Red Cross)
  geom_point(aes(x = true_value, color = "True"), 
             shape = 4, size = 5, stroke = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Hierarchical" = "midnightblue", 
                                "Unpooled" = "grey60", 
                                "True" = "red")) +
  labs(title = "Hyperparameter Recovery & Partial Pooling", 
       subtitle = "Comparing Hierarchical Posteriors (Mean ± 90% CI), Unpooled Sample Stats, and True Values",
       x = "Parameter Value", y = NULL, color = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

# 6. Combine with patchwork
(p_pop_rho | p_pop_kappa) / p_hyper +
  plot_layout(heights = c(1.5, 1)) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Population-Level Recovery and Variance Shrinkage",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"))
  )









Variance Shrinkage: The top image demonstrates that the hierarchical model accurately recovers the true, tighter variance of the population distributions. In contrast, the unpooled sample statistics are artificially wide, overestimating the true variance.


Code
# 1. unpooled_ests was computed in the previous chunk

# 2. Extract MULTILEVEL (partially pooled) posterior means and join
recovery_ml <- agent_params_ml |>
  mutate(
    est_rho   = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_ml[[paste0("rho[", .x, "]")]])),
    est_kappa = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_ml[[paste0("kappa[", .x, "]")]])),
    est_wd    = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_ml[[paste0("weight_direct[", .x, "]")]])),
    est_ws    = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_ml[[paste0("weight_social[", .x, "]")]]))
  ) |>
  left_join(unpooled_ests, by = "agent_id")

# 3. Calculate population means (the "center of gravity")
pop_rho_true   <- plogis(pop_logit_rho_mean)
pop_kappa_true <- exp(pop_log_kappa_mean)
pop_wd_true    <- pop_rho_true * pop_kappa_true
pop_ws_true    <- (1 - pop_rho_true) * pop_kappa_true

# 4. Determine a common maximum scale for wd and ws plots
max_w <- max(c(recovery_ml$weight_direct, recovery_ml$est_wd, recovery_ml$unpooled_wd,
               recovery_ml$weight_social, recovery_ml$est_ws, recovery_ml$unpooled_ws)) * 1.05

# 5. Build plots: Arrow from Unpooled (hollow) to Pooled (solid)
p_rho <- ggplot(recovery_ml, aes(x = rho)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = pop_rho_true, linetype = "dotted", colour = "black", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = rho, y = unpooled_rho, yend = est_rho), 
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm")), colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(aes(y = unpooled_rho), shape = 1, size = 2.5, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(aes(y = est_rho), size = 3, alpha = 0.9, colour = "#4575b4") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 1), ylim = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = "Recovery: ρ (relative allocation)", x = "True ρ", y = "Estimated ρ") 

p_kappa <- ggplot(recovery_ml, aes(x = kappa)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = pop_kappa_true, linetype = "dotted", colour = "black", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = kappa, y = unpooled_kappa, yend = est_kappa), 
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm")), colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(aes(y = unpooled_kappa), shape = 1, size = 2.5, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(aes(y = est_kappa), size = 3, alpha = 0.9, colour = "#d73027") +
  labs(title = "Recovery: κ (total evidence scaling)", x = "True κ", y = "Estimated κ") 

p_wd <- ggplot(recovery_ml, aes(x = weight_direct)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = pop_wd_true, linetype = "dotted", colour = "black", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = weight_direct, y = unpooled_wd, yend = est_wd), 
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm")), colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(aes(y = unpooled_wd), shape = 1, size = 2.5, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(aes(y = est_wd), size = 3, alpha = 0.9, colour = "#4daf4a") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, max_w), ylim = c(0, max_w)) +
  labs(title = "Recovery: w_d (derived)", x = "True w_d", y = "Estimated w_d")

p_ws <- ggplot(recovery_ml, aes(x = weight_social)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = pop_ws_true, linetype = "dotted", colour = "black", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = weight_social, y = unpooled_ws, yend = est_ws), 
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm")), colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(aes(y = unpooled_ws), shape = 1, size = 2.5, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(aes(y = est_ws), size = 3, alpha = 0.9, colour = "#984ea3") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, max_w), ylim = c(0, max_w)) +
  labs(title = "Recovery: w_s (derived)", x = "True w_s", y = "Estimated w_s")

(p_rho | p_kappa) / (p_wd | p_ws) +
  plot_annotation(
    title    = "Multilevel WBA Parameter Recovery & Shrinkage",
    subtitle = "Arrows trace shrinkage from Unpooled MAP estimates (hollow) to Multilevel Posterior Means (solid).\nNotice how pooling pulls extreme estimates toward the population mean (dotted horizontal lines)."
  )









Look for three features. First, \(\rho\) recovery should be tight — this parameter is well-identified from conflict trials, just as in the single-agent case. Second, \(\kappa\) recovery will be noisier — the total evidence scale is harder to pin down from binary outcomes. Third, agents at the extremes should be pulled toward the centre relative to their true values. This is partial pooling in action: the hierarchical prior regularises extreme estimates. The strength of this shrinkage depends on how informative each agent’s data are relative to the population distribution.

title: 12  Bayesian Models of Cognition
text:
12.22 Simulation-Based Calibration Checks (SBC): Multilevel Models
SBC is more critical for multilevel models than for single-agent models. The hierarchical posterior geometry — with its potential funnels, correlations, and partial-pooling dynamics — creates subtle failure modes that standard MCMC diagnostics may miss. We run SBC for both the ML-WBA and ML-PBA.


Code
n_sbc_ml <- 200  # Reduced from single-agent count due to computational cost

if (regenerate_simulations || !all(file.exists(c("simmodels/ch10_sbc_ml_wba.rds", "simmodels/ch10_sbc_ml_pba.rds")))) {
  
  # ── ML-WBA SBC Generator ──────────────────────────────────────────
  sbc_gen_ml_wba <- SBC_generator_function(
    function(n_agents_sbc, ev, n_reps) {
      J <- n_agents_sbc
      
      # Draw population parameters from priors
      mu_1     <- rnorm(1, 0, 1.5)       # logit_rho mean
      mu_2     <- rnorm(1, log(2), 0.5)   # log_kappa mean
      sigma_1  <- rexp(1, 2)
      sigma_2  <- rexp(1, 2)
      
      # LKJ(2) correlation: draw from marginal Beta, rescale
      r_raw    <- rbeta(1, 2, 2)
      Omega_12 <- 2 * r_raw - 1
      
      # Construct Cholesky of correlation matrix
      L11 <- 1; L21 <- Omega_12; L22 <- sqrt(1 - Omega_12^2)
      
      # Draw individual parameters (NCP)
      z <- matrix(rnorm(2 * J), nrow = 2)
      theta_raw <- diag(c(sigma_1, sigma_2)) %*% rbind(
        L11 * z[1,],
        L21 * z[1,] + L22 * z[2,]
      )
      
      rho_j   <- plogis(mu_1 + theta_raw[1,])
      kappa_j <- exp(mu_2 + theta_raw[2,])
      wd_j    <- rho_j * kappa_j
      ws_j    <- (1 - rho_j) * kappa_j
      
      # Generate trial data for each agent
      all_data <- map_dfr(seq_len(J), function(j) {
        agent_ev <- ev |> slice(rep(seq_len(n()), times = n_reps))
        N_j <- nrow(agent_ev)
        
        alpha_p <- 0.5 + wd_j[j] * agent_ev$blue1 + ws_j[j] * agent_ev$blue2
        beta_p  <- 0.5 + wd_j[j] * (agent_ev$total1 - agent_ev$blue1) +
                         ws_j[j] * (agent_ev$total2 - agent_ev$blue2)
        p_blue  <- rbeta(N_j, alpha_p, beta_p)
        
        agent_ev |> mutate(
          agent_id = j,
          choice   = rbinom(N_j, 1, p_blue)
        )
      })
      
      list(
        variables = list(
          `mu[1]`      = mu_1,
          `mu[2]`      = mu_2,
          `sigma[1]`   = sigma_1,
          `sigma[2]`   = sigma_2,
          `Omega[1,2]` = Omega_12
        ),
        generated = list(
          N        = nrow(all_data),
          J        = J,
          agent_id = all_data$agent_id,
          choice   = all_data$choice,
          blue1    = all_data$blue1,
          total1   = all_data$total1,
          blue2    = all_data$blue2,
          total2   = all_data$total2
        )
      )
    },
    n_agents_sbc = 10,  # Fewer agents for SBC speed
    ev           = evidence_combinations,
    n_reps       = 2
  )
  
  sbc_datasets_ml_wba <- generate_datasets(sbc_gen_ml_wba, n_sbc_ml)
  
  sbc_backend_ml_wba <- SBC::SBC_backend_cmdstan_sample(
    mod_ml_wba,
    chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500, iter_sampling = 500,
    parallel_chains = 2, adapt_delta = 0.95,
    refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
  )
  
  sbc_results_ml_wba <- compute_SBC(sbc_datasets_ml_wba, sbc_backend_ml_wba,
                                     keep_fits = FALSE)
  saveRDS(sbc_results_ml_wba, "simmodels/ch10_sbc_ml_wba.rds")

  # ── ML-PBA SBC Generator ──────────────────────────────────────────
  sbc_gen_ml_pba <- SBC_generator_function(
    function(n_agents_sbc, ev, n_reps) {
      J <- n_agents_sbc
      
      mu_logit_p    <- rnorm(1, 0, 1.5)
      sigma_logit_p <- rexp(1, 2)
      z_p           <- rnorm(J)
      p_j           <- plogis(mu_logit_p + sigma_logit_p * z_p)
      
      all_data <- map_dfr(seq_len(J), function(j) {
        agent_ev <- ev |> slice(rep(seq_len(n()), times = n_reps))
        N_j <- nrow(agent_ev)
        
        alpha_p <- 0.5 + p_j[j] * agent_ev$blue1 + (1 - p_j[j]) * agent_ev$blue2
        beta_p  <- 0.5 + p_j[j] * (agent_ev$total1 - agent_ev$blue1) +
                         (1 - p_j[j]) * (agent_ev$total2 - agent_ev$blue2)
        p_blue  <- rbeta(N_j, alpha_p, beta_p)
        
        agent_ev |> mutate(
          agent_id = j,
          choice   = rbinom(N_j, 1, p_blue)
        )
      })
      
      list(
        variables = list(
          mu_logit_p    = mu_logit_p,
          sigma_logit_p = sigma_logit_p
        ),
        generated = list(
          N        = nrow(all_data),
          J        = J,
          agent_id = all_data$agent_id,
          choice   = all_data$choice,
          blue1    = all_data$blue1,
          total1   = all_data$total1,
          blue2    = all_data$blue2,
          total2   = all_data$total2
        )
      )
    },
    n_agents_sbc = 10,
    ev           = evidence_combinations,
    n_reps       = 2
  )
  
  sbc_datasets_ml_pba <- generate_datasets(sbc_gen_ml_pba, n_sbc_ml)
  
  sbc_backend_ml_pba <- SBC::SBC_backend_cmdstan_sample(
    mod_ml_pba,
    chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500, iter_sampling = 500,
    parallel_chains = 2, adapt_delta = 0.95,
    refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
  )
  
  sbc_results_ml_pba <- compute_SBC(sbc_datasets_ml_pba, sbc_backend_ml_pba,
                                     keep_fits = FALSE)
  saveRDS(sbc_results_ml_pba, "simmodels/ch10_sbc_ml_pba.rds")
  
} else {
  sbc_results_ml_wba <- readRDS("simmodels/ch10_sbc_ml_wba.rds")
  sbc_results_ml_pba <- readRDS("simmodels/ch10_sbc_ml_pba.rds")
}

# Plot SBC calibration
p_sbc_ml_wba <- SBC::plot_ecdf_diff(
  sbc_results_ml_wba,
  variables = c("mu[1]", "mu[2]", "sigma[1]", "sigma[2]", "Omega[1,2]")
) + labs(title = "SBC Calibration: ML-WBA (population parameters)",
         subtitle = paste0("N = ", n_sbc_ml, " simulations. Lines inside bands = calibrated."))

p_sbc_ml_pba <- SBC::plot_ecdf_diff(
  sbc_results_ml_pba,
  variables = c("mu_logit_p", "sigma_logit_p")
) + labs(title = "SBC Calibration: ML-PBA (population parameters)",
         subtitle = paste0("N = ", n_sbc_ml, " simulations."))

p_sbc_ml_wba / p_sbc_ml_pba









If any parameter’s ECDF difference line escapes the grey bands, the posterior for that parameter is systematically miscalibrated. Common failure modes for multilevel models: (1) \(\sigma\) dipping below the band near 0, indicating the hierarchical prior’s boundary repulsion is too weak; (2) the correlation \(\Omega_{1,2}\) showing asymmetric miscalibration, indicating the LKJ prior interacts poorly with the NCP geometry. If SBC passes, we have a global certificate that the Stan sampler is unbiased across the entire joint prior space.

12.22.1 Global Parameter Recovery from SBC


Code
ml_wba_sbc_recovery <- sbc_results_ml_wba$stats |>
  filter(variable %in% c("mu[1]", "mu[2]", "sigma[1]", "sigma[2]", "Omega[1,2]")) |>
  mutate(model = "ML-WBA")

ml_pba_sbc_recovery <- sbc_results_ml_pba$stats |>
  filter(variable %in% c("mu_logit_p", "sigma_logit_p")) |>
  mutate(model = "ML-PBA")

sbc_ml_recovery_df <- bind_rows(ml_wba_sbc_recovery, ml_pba_sbc_recovery)

ggplot(sbc_ml_recovery_df, aes(x = simulated_value, y = median)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = q5, ymax = q95), alpha = 0.2, width = 0) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 1.5, color = "#4575b4") +
  facet_wrap(model ~ variable, scales = "free", ncol = 4) +
  labs(
    title = "Global Parameter Recovery: Multilevel SBC",
    subtitle = "Each point = one simulated population. Error bars = 90% CI.",
    x = "True (simulated) value",
    y = "Posterior median"
  ) +
  theme(strip.background = element_rect(fill = "gray95", color = NA))

title: 12  Bayesian Models of Cognition
text:
12.23 Multilevel Model Comparison: Three-Way LOO
Following the procedure from the single-agent comparison, we compare all three multilevel models via PSIS-LOO. Because the simulated population has agents with varying weights, we expect the ML-WBA to outperform the ML-SBA. Whether the ML-PBA or ML-WBA wins depends on whether the population s \(\kappa\) variation provides sufficient predictive benefit to offset the additional complexity.


Code
loo_ml_sba <- loo(fit_ml_sba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
loo_ml_pba <- loo(fit_ml_pba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
loo_ml_wba <- loo(fit_ml_wba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))

cat("=== Multilevel Model Comparison: ML-SBA vs. ML-PBA vs. ML-WBA ===\n")


=== Multilevel Model Comparison: ML-SBA vs. ML-PBA vs. ML-WBA ===


Code
ml_comparison <- loo_compare(
  list("ML-SBA" = loo_ml_sba, "ML-PBA" = loo_ml_pba, "ML-WBA" = loo_ml_wba)
)
print(ml_comparison)


       elpd_diff se_diff
ML-SBA  0.0       0.0   
ML-WBA -1.9       1.0   
ML-PBA -2.7       2.0   


Code
ml_weights <- loo_model_weights(
  list("ML-SBA" = loo_ml_sba, "ML-PBA" = loo_ml_pba, "ML-WBA" = loo_ml_wba),
  method = "stacking"
)
cat("\nBayesian stacking weights:\n")



Bayesian stacking weights:


Code
print(ml_weights)


Method: stacking
------
       weight
ML-SBA 0.987 
ML-PBA 0.013 
ML-WBA 0.000 


Because the simulated agents were drawn from a population with varying \(\rho\) and \(\kappa\) (not all equal to 1), the ML-WBA should outperform the ML-SBA. The ELPD difference quantifies the predictive cost of assuming all agents are perfect Bayesians when they are not. However, given we set the simulation to be close to the SBA (balanced weighting), the ML-SBA ends up winning.
Note that this is trial-level LOO: it measures generalisation to new trials from known agents. For the clinically relevant question — can we predict a new participant’s behaviour from the population distribution alone? — we would need agent-level K-fold cross-validation, as discussed in Chapter 8.

12.23.1 Visualising Pointwise ELPD Differences
To understand where the ML-WBA gains its advantage over the ML-SBA, we plot pointwise ELPD differences as a function of evidence conflict — the same diagnostic technique from the single-agent section.


Code
pw_ml <- tibble(
  obs       = seq_len(stan_data_ml$N),
  agent_id  = stan_data_ml$agent_id,
  blue1     = stan_data_ml$blue1,
  blue2     = stan_data_ml$blue2,
  elpd_wba  = loo_ml_wba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"],
  elpd_sba  = loo_ml_sba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"],
  elpd_pba  = loo_ml_pba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"],
  elpd_diff_wba_sba = elpd_wba - elpd_sba,
  elpd_diff_wba_pba = elpd_wba - elpd_pba
) |>
  left_join(
    agent_params_ml |> dplyr::select(agent_id, rho, kappa),
    by = "agent_id"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    prop_direct       = blue1 / 8,
    prop_social       = blue2 / 3,
    evidence_conflict = abs(prop_direct - prop_social),
    rho_group = cut(rho, breaks = c(0, 0.35, 0.65, 1),
                    labels = c("Social-leaning", "Balanced", "Direct-leaning"))
  )

# WBA vs SBA, coloured by agent type
ggplot(pw_ml, aes(x = evidence_conflict, y = elpd_diff_wba_sba)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(aes(colour = rho_group), size = 1.5, alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "gam", formula = y ~ s(x, bs = "cs"),
              colour = "midnightblue", linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ rho_group) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("Social-leaning" = "#377eb8",
               "Balanced"       = "#4daf4a",
               "Direct-leaning" = "#e41a1c")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Pointwise ELPD: ML-WBA vs. ML-SBA",
    subtitle = "Faceted by agent type. Advantage grows with evidence conflict for non-balanced agents.",
    x = "Evidence Conflict |prop_direct − prop_social|",
    y = "ELPD Difference (ML-WBA − ML-SBA)",
    colour = "Agent type"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: 12  Bayesian Models of Cognition
text:
12.24 Model Recovery: Multilevel Level
Model recovery at the multilevel level asks: if data are generated by a specific multilevel model, can our comparison pipeline correctly identify it? We test three scenarios, paralleling the single-agent analysis.

12.24.1 Specific Scenarios
Before the full simulation, we examine three instructive scenarios:

SBA-Mimic Population: All agents have \(\rho = 0.5, \kappa = 2.0\) (i.e., \(w_d = w_s = 1\)) with zero individual variation. The population is the SBA.
PBA Population: Agents vary in \(\rho\) (drawn from a population) but all have \(\kappa = 1.0\). The total evidence budget is fixed; only allocation varies.
Full WBA Population: Agents vary in both \(\rho\) and \(\kappa\). The full hierarchical model is needed.



Code
# Helper: generate multilevel data from a parameter specification
generate_ml_data <- function(agent_rhos, agent_kappas, ev, n_reps = 2) {
  J <- length(agent_rhos)
  all_data <- map_dfr(seq_len(J), function(j) {
    wd <- agent_rhos[j] * agent_kappas[j]
    ws <- (1 - agent_rhos[j]) * agent_kappas[j]
    generate_agent_decisions(wd, ws, ev, n_samples = n_reps) |>
      mutate(agent_id = j, total1 = 8L, total2 = 3L)
  })
  list(
    N = nrow(all_data), J = J,
    agent_id   = all_data$agent_id,
    choice     = all_data$choice,
    N_total    = nrow(all_data),
    N_subjects = J,
    subj_id    = all_data$agent_id,
    y          = all_data$choice,
    blue1      = all_data$blue1,
    total1     = all_data$total1,
    blue2      = all_data$blue2,
    total2     = all_data$total2
  )
}

J_scen <- 15

# Scenario 1: SBA-Mimic (no individual variation)
scen1_data <- generate_ml_data(
  agent_rhos   = rep(0.5, J_scen),
  agent_kappas = rep(2.0, J_scen),
  ev = evidence_combinations
)

# Scenario 2: PBA Population (rho varies, kappa = 1)
scen2_rhos <- plogis(rnorm(J_scen, 0.5, 0.5))
scen2_data <- generate_ml_data(
  agent_rhos   = scen2_rhos,
  agent_kappas = rep(1.0, J_scen),
  ev = evidence_combinations
)

# Scenario 3: Full WBA population (both vary)
scen3_draws <- MASS::mvrnorm(J_scen, mu = c(0.3, log(2.5)),
                              Sigma = matrix(c(0.5, -0.15, -0.15, 0.2), 2))
scen3_data <- generate_ml_data(
  agent_rhos   = plogis(scen3_draws[, 1]),
  agent_kappas = exp(scen3_draws[, 2]),
  ev = evidence_combinations
)




Code
fit_ml_scenario <- function(stan_data, label) {
  cat(sprintf("\n--- Fitting scenario: %s ---\n", label))

  sba <- mod_ml_sba$sample(
    data = stan_data, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 1000,
    iter_sampling = 1000, adapt_delta = 0.9,
    refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
  )
  pba <- mod_ml_pba$sample(
    data = stan_data, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 1000,
    iter_sampling = 1000, adapt_delta = 0.95,
    refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
  )
  wba <- mod_ml_wba$sample(
    data = stan_data, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 1000,
    iter_sampling = 1000, adapt_delta = 0.95,
    refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
  )

  # Return pre-computed LOO objects, not the raw fits
  list(
    SBA = loo(sba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix")),
    PBA = loo(pba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix")),
    WBA = loo(wba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
  )
}

if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists("simmodels/ch10_ml_scenario_loos.rds")) {
  loos_scen1 <- fit_ml_scenario(scen1_data, "SBA-Mimic Population")
  loos_scen2 <- fit_ml_scenario(scen2_data, "PBA Population")
  loos_scen3 <- fit_ml_scenario(scen3_data, "Full WBA Population")
  saveRDS(list(s1 = loos_scen1, s2 = loos_scen2, s3 = loos_scen3),
          "simmodels/ch10_ml_scenario_loos.rds")
} else {
  loos_all   <- readRDS("simmodels/ch10_ml_scenario_loos.rds")
  loos_scen1 <- loos_all$s1
  loos_scen2 <- loos_all$s2
  loos_scen3 <- loos_all$s3
}




Code
compute_ml_scenario <- function(loos, scenario_name) {
  cat(sprintf("\n=== %s ===\n", scenario_name))
  comp <- loo_compare(loos)
  print(comp)

  w <- loo_model_weights(loos, method = "stacking")
  cat("Stacking weights:\n")
  print(round(w, 3))

  list(loos = loos, weights = w, comparison = comp)
}

res_scen1 <- compute_ml_scenario(loos_scen1, "Scenario 1: SBA-Mimic Population")

Code
# Build summary table
extract_ml_loo <- function(res, name) {
  comp <- as_tibble(res$comparison, rownames = "Model") |>
    dplyr::select(Model, elpd_diff, se_diff)
  w <- tibble(Model = names(res$weights),
              weight = as.numeric(res$weights))
  comp |> left_join(w, by = "Model") |> mutate(Scenario = name)
}

ml_scenario_summary <- bind_rows(
  extract_ml_loo(res_scen1, "1. SBA-Mimic"),
  extract_ml_loo(res_scen2, "2. PBA Population"),
  extract_ml_loo(res_scen3, "3. Full WBA")
) |>
  mutate(
    Model    = factor(Model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA")),
    Scenario = factor(Scenario)
  )

# ELPD differences
p_ml_elpd <- ggplot(ml_scenario_summary,
                     aes(x = Model, y = elpd_diff, colour = Model)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = elpd_diff - se_diff,
                       ymax = elpd_diff + se_diff),
                  size = 0.8, linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ Scenario) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Multilevel ELPD Differences", y = "ELPD Difference", x = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11))

# Stacking weights
p_ml_weight <- ggplot(ml_scenario_summary,
                       aes(x = Model, y = weight, fill = Model)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85, width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(weight, accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  facet_wrap(~ Scenario) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1.2)) +
  labs(title = "Multilevel Stacking Weights", y = "Stacking Weight", x = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "none", strip.text = element_blank())

p_ml_elpd / p_ml_weight +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Multilevel Model Recovery: Scenario Case Studies",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"))
  )









The expected pattern mirrors the single-agent case. In Scenario 1, the SBA-mimic population has no individual variation in weights, so the ML-SBA (zero parameters) should win or tie with the ML-PBA and ML-WBA — complexity is penalised. In Scenario 2, the PBA population requires individual variation in \(\rho\) but not in \(\kappa\), so the ML-PBA should win. In Scenario 3, the full WBA population needs both \(\rho\) and \(\kappa\) variation, and the ML-WBA should dominate.


12.24.2 LOO Pointwise Diagnostics Across Scenarios


Code
make_ml_pw <- function(loos, stan_data, scenario_name, ref_model = "SBA") {
  elpd_wba <- loos$WBA$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"]
  elpd_ref <- loos[[ref_model]]$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"]
  
  tibble(
    obs       = seq_len(stan_data$N),
    blue1     = stan_data$blue1,
    blue2     = stan_data$blue2,
    elpd_diff = elpd_wba - elpd_ref,
    Scenario  = scenario_name
  ) |>
    mutate(
      evidence_conflict = abs(blue1 / 8 - blue2 / 3)
    )
}

ml_pw <- bind_rows(
  make_ml_pw(res_scen1$loos, scen1_data,
             "1. SBA-Mimic\n(ML-WBA vs ML-SBA)", "SBA"),
  make_ml_pw(res_scen2$loos, scen2_data,
             "2. PBA Population\n(ML-WBA vs ML-PBA)", "PBA"),
  make_ml_pw(res_scen3$loos, scen3_data,
             "3. Full WBA\n(ML-WBA vs ML-PBA)", "PBA")
)

ggplot(ml_pw, aes(x = evidence_conflict, y = elpd_diff)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(aes(colour = elpd_diff > 0), size = 1.5, alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "gam", formula = y ~ s(x, bs = "cs"),
              colour = "midnightblue", linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ Scenario) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("FALSE" = "firebrick", "TRUE" = "steelblue"),
    labels = c("FALSE" = "Favours simpler model", "TRUE" = "Favours ML-WBA"),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Multilevel Pointwise ELPD: Where Does Complexity Help?",
    subtitle = "In mimicking scenarios (left), the WBA loses by a constant complexity penalty.\nIn the full WBA population (right), advantage emerges on high-conflict trials.",
    x = "Evidence Conflict |prop_direct − prop_social|",
    y = "ELPD Difference"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")











12.24.3 Full Model Recovery Simulation
We now scale up to a full model recovery simulation at the multilevel level. For each of the three generating models (ML-SBA, ML-PBA, ML-WBA), we generate multiple synthetic populations, cross-fit all three candidates, and examine the confusion matrix and calibration.


Code
n_ml_sims <- 30  # Per generating model
J_recovery <- 12  # Agents per simulated population

if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists("simmodels/ch10_ml_model_recovery.rds")) {
  
  ml_recovery_results <- list()
  counter <- 0
  
  for (true_model in c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA")) {
    for (sim_id in seq_len(n_ml_sims)) {
      counter <- counter + 1
      
      # Generate population parameters
      if (true_model == "SBA") {
        rhos   <- rep(0.5, J_recovery)
        kappas <- rep(2.0, J_recovery)
      } else if (true_model == "PBA") {
        rhos   <- plogis(rnorm(J_recovery, 0, 0.6))
        kappas <- rep(1.0, J_recovery)
      } else {
        draws_raw <- MASS::mvrnorm(
          J_recovery,
          mu    = c(0, log(2)),
          Sigma = matrix(c(0.4, -0.1, -0.1, 0.15), 2)
        )
        rhos   <- plogis(draws_raw[, 1])
        kappas <- exp(draws_raw[, 2])
      }
      
      sim_stan <- generate_ml_data(rhos, kappas, evidence_combinations, n_reps = 2)
      
      # Fit all three models (reduced iterations for speed)
      f_sba <- mod_ml_sba$sample(
        data = sim_stan, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500,
        iter_sampling = 500, adapt_delta = 0.9,
        refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
      )
      f_pba <- mod_ml_pba$sample(
        data = sim_stan, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500,
        iter_sampling = 500, adapt_delta = 0.95,
        refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
      )
      f_wba <- mod_ml_wba$sample(
        data = sim_stan, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500,
        iter_sampling = 500, adapt_delta = 0.95,
        refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
      )
      
      # Compute LOO and stacking weights
      l_sba <- loo(f_sba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
      l_pba <- loo(f_pba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
      l_wba <- loo(f_wba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
      
      w <- tryCatch(
        loo_model_weights(
          list(SBA = l_sba, PBA = l_pba, WBA = l_wba),
          method = "stacking"
        ),
        error = function(e) c(SBA = 1/3, PBA = 1/3, WBA = 1/3)
      )
      
      ml_recovery_results[[counter]] <- tibble(
        sim_id     = sim_id,
        true_model = true_model,
        weight_SBA = as.numeric(w["SBA"]),
        weight_PBA = as.numeric(w["PBA"]),
        weight_WBA = as.numeric(w["WBA"]),
        mean_true_rho   = mean(rhos),
        sd_true_rho     = sd(rhos),
        mean_true_kappa = mean(kappas),
        sd_true_kappa   = sd(kappas)
      )
      
      if (counter %% 10 == 0) {
        cat(sprintf("  Completed %d / %d simulations\n", counter, 3 * n_ml_sims))
      }
    }
  }
  
  ml_recovery_df <- bind_rows(ml_recovery_results)
  saveRDS(ml_recovery_df, "simmodels/ch10_ml_model_recovery.rds")
  
} else {
  ml_recovery_df <- readRDS("simmodels/ch10_ml_model_recovery.rds")
}




12.24.4 Confusion Matrix


Code
# Binary confusion matrix (max-weight winner)
ml_binary <- ml_recovery_df |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("weight_"),
               names_to = "predicted_model", values_to = "weight") |>
  mutate(predicted_model = str_remove(predicted_model, "weight_")) |>
  group_by(sim_id, true_model) |>
  slice_max(weight, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  count(true_model, predicted_model) |>
  group_by(true_model) |>
  mutate(proportion = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    true_model      = factor(true_model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA")),
    predicted_model = factor(predicted_model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA"))
  ) |>
  complete(true_model, predicted_model, fill = list(n = 0, proportion = 0))

ml_binary |>
  dplyr::select(-n) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = predicted_model, values_from = proportion) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2,
               caption = "Multilevel Binary Confusion Matrix: Win Rate")

# Probabilistic confusion matrix (average stacking weights)
ml_prob_confusion <- ml_recovery_df |>
  group_by(true_model) |>

    Recovered_PBA = mean(weight_PBA),

  mutate(true_model = factor(true_model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA")))

Code
ml_prob_confusion |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("Recovered"),
               names_to = "fitted_model", values_to = "weight") |>
  mutate(fitted_model = factor(str_remove(fitted_model, "Recovered_"),
                                levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA"))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fitted_model, y = true_model, fill = weight)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(weight, accuracy = 1)),
            size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "mako", direction = -1, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev) +
  labs(
    title = "Multilevel Model Recovery: Average Stacking Weights",
    x = "Candidate Model",
    y = "True Generating Model",
    fill = "Avg. Weight"
  ) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())









The expected pattern: the diagonal should dominate. Because the models are nested (SBA \(\subset\) PBA \(\subset\) WBA), Occam’s razor should produce asymmetric off-diagonal errors — the WBA should sometimes be classified as PBA or SBA when its parameters mimic simpler models, but the SBA should rarely be classified as WBA when extra parameters are unnecessary. This asymmetry is a feature of the comparison pipeline, not a bug.


12.24.5 Model Comparison Calibration
A well-calibrated comparison procedure assigns a stacking weight of, say, 0.7 to a model that turns out to be the true generator roughly 70% of the time. We assess this by binning stacking weights and computing the empirical frequency with which the candidate was the true model.


Code
ml_cal_long <- ml_recovery_df |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("weight_"),
               names_to = "candidate", values_to = "stacking_weight") |>
  mutate(
    candidate = str_remove(candidate, "weight_"),
    is_true   = as.integer(candidate == true_model)
  )

n_bins <- 8
ml_cal_binned <- ml_cal_long |>
  mutate(bin = cut(stacking_weight,
                   breaks = seq(0, 1, length.out = n_bins + 1),
                   include.lowest = TRUE)) |>
  group_by(candidate, bin) |>
  summarise(
    bin_mid    = mean(stacking_weight),
    observed   = mean(is_true),
    N_datasets = n(),
    .groups    = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    SE    = sqrt((observed * (1 - observed)) / pmax(N_datasets, 1)),
    Lower = pmax(0, observed - 1.96 * SE),
    Upper = pmin(1, observed + 1.96 * SE)
  )

ggplot(ml_cal_binned, aes(x = bin_mid, y = observed, colour = candidate)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Lower, ymax = Upper, fill = candidate),
              alpha = 0.15, colour = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Multilevel Model Comparison Calibration",
    subtitle = "Points on the diagonal = well-calibrated stacking weights.\nRibbons = 95% CI; wider ribbons reflect fewer datasets in that bin.",
    x = "Mean Stacking Weight (Binned)",
    y = "Empirical Frequency (True Generating Model)",
    colour = "Candidate", fill = "Candidate"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")









Points on the diagonal indicate perfect calibration: a stacking weight of 0.7 means the model is the true generator 70% of the time. A curve bowed above the diagonal signals underconfidence (the procedure hedges too much); a curve below signals overconfidence. The multilevel comparison has additional sources of uncertainty relative to the single-agent case — partial pooling, correlation estimation, and funnel geometry — so some additional calibration noise is expected.

title: 12  Bayesian Models of Cognition
section: 12.25 Summary: What the Multilevel Pipeline Adds
text:
12.25 Summary: What the Multilevel Pipeline Adds
The multilevel extension answers the population-level question: how do people in general weight evidence, and how much do they vary? By using the reparameterised \((\rho_j, \kappa_j)\) parameterisation — not the raw \((w_d, w_s)\) shown to be unidentifiable in the single-agent case — we preserve the identifiability gains while gaining partial pooling.
The full pipeline mirrors the single-agent workflow:

Model quality: Diagnostics, trace plots, and summary statistics confirmed clean sampling for all three multilevel models.
Parameter recovery: Individual \(\rho\) values recover well (identified from conflict trials); \(\kappa\) is noisier but partially regularised by the hierarchical prior. Partial pooling pulls extreme agents toward the population mean, as expected.
SBC certification: The multilevel SBC established that the Stan sampler is globally unbiased for the ML-WBA and ML-PBA population parameters — a critical check given the more complex posterior geometry.
Model comparison: The three-way LOO comparison with pointwise ELPD visualizations showed where each model’s advantage lies (high-conflict trials for non-balanced populations). Scenario case studies demonstrated the nested Occam’s razor effect: simpler populations are correctly attributed to simpler models.
Model recovery: The confusion matrix and calibration curves quantified the comparison procedure’s reliability across many simulated populations, confirming appropriate asymmetric error patterns from the nested model structure.

The key lesson: the reparameterisation that solved the single-agent identifiability problem is equally essential at the multilevel level. The hierarchical prior partially constrains the scale, but it does not eliminate the ridge in each agent’s conditional likelihood. Only by separating allocation (\(\rho\)) from scaling (\(\kappa\)) can we obtain meaningful individual-level parameter estimates — and by extension, meaningful population-level summaries of how people vary.

title: 12  Bayesian Models of Cognition
section: 12.26 Mixed Strategies: When the Population Is Heterogeneous
text:
12.26 Mixed Strategies: When the Population Is Heterogeneous
Every analysis above assumed a homogeneous population: all agents were drawn from the same generative model. In practice, this assumption is almost certainly wrong. A real experimental sample plausibly contains people who use qualitatively different strategies — some may integrate evidence at face value (SBA-like), while others systematically overweight social or direct evidence (WBA-like). What happens to our pipeline when the population is a genuine mixture?
This section merges data from two generating models — half the participants behave as Simple Bayesian Agents, half as Weighted Bayesian Agents with a strong social bias — and examines whether the hierarchical models and the comparison pipeline can still produce useful inference.
Because no single hierarchical model can correctly represent a bimodal population distribution, we should not expect population-level parameters to be meaningful. The scientific question shifts from “what does the average person do?” to “which strategy does each person use?” This motivates a focus on individual-level parameter recovery and per-agent model comparison, skipping the usual population-level parameter interpretation.

12.26.1 Generating the Mixed Population


Code
set.seed(2026)
J_mix <- 40  # 20 SBA + 20 WBA agents

# --- SBA agents: strict Bayesian integration (wd = 1, ws = 1) ---
sba_rhos   <- rep(0.5, 20)
sba_kappas <- rep(2.0, 20)

# --- WBA agents: strong social bias, higher total sensitivity ---
# Draw from a population distribution to add realistic variation
wba_raw <- MASS::mvrnorm(
  20,
  mu    = c(qlogis(0.25), log(2.5)),   # rho ≈ 0.25, kappa ≈ 2.5
  Sigma = matrix(c(0.25, -0.08, -0.08, 0.12), 2)
)
wba_rhos   <- plogis(wba_raw[, 1])
wba_kappas <- exp(wba_raw[, 2])

# Combine and shuffle so agent IDs don't reveal strategy
agent_order   <- sample(J_mix)
all_rhos      <- c(sba_rhos, wba_rhos)[agent_order]
all_kappas    <- c(sba_kappas, wba_kappas)[agent_order]
true_strategy <- c(rep("SBA", 20), rep("WBA", 20))[agent_order]

# Generate trial-level data
mixed_stan_data <- generate_ml_data(all_rhos, all_kappas,
                                     evidence_combinations, n_reps = 2)

# Store ground truth for recovery plots
mixed_truth <- tibble(
  agent_id       = seq_len(J_mix),
  true_rho       = all_rhos,
  true_kappa     = all_kappas,
  true_wd        = all_rhos * all_kappas,
  true_ws        = (1 - all_rhos) * all_kappas,
  true_strategy  = true_strategy
)

cat(sprintf("Mixed population: %d agents (%d SBA + %d WBA)\n",
            J_mix, sum(true_strategy == "SBA"), sum(true_strategy == "WBA")))


Mixed population: 40 agents (20 SBA + 20 WBA)


Code
cat(sprintf("Total trials: %d | Trials per agent: %d\n",
            mixed_stan_data$N, mixed_stan_data$N / J_mix))


Total trials: 2880 | Trials per agent: 72




Code
ggplot(mixed_truth, aes(x = true_wd, y = true_ws,
                         colour = true_strategy, shape = true_strategy)) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = agent_id), nudge_x = 0.04, size = 3, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("SBA" = 16, "WBA" = 17)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title    = "Mixed Population: True Agent Parameters",
    subtitle = "SBA agents cluster at (1, 1); WBA agents are socially biased with higher total sensitivity",
    x        = expression("True " * w[d]),
    y        = expression("True " * w[s]),
    colour   = "Strategy", shape = "Strategy"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")









The SBA agents form a tight cluster at \((w_d, w_s) = (1, 1)\) — they are all identical by construction. The WBA agents scatter around \((w_d, w_s) \approx (0.6, 1.9)\), each with their own idiosyncratic weights drawn from the population distribution. No single Gaussian population on the \((\text{logit}(\rho), \log(\kappa))\) scale can represent this bimodal structure.


12.26.2 Fitting All Three Multilevel Models


Code
if (regenerate_simulations || !all(file.exists(c("simmodels/ch10_fit_mix_sba.rds", "simmodels/ch10_fit_mix_pba.rds", "simmodels/ch10_fit_mix_wba.rds")))) {
  
  fit_mix_sba <- mod_ml_sba$sample(
    data = mixed_stan_data, chains = 4, parallel_chains = 4,
    iter_warmup = 1000, iter_sampling = 1000,
    adapt_delta = 0.9, refresh = 0
  )

  fit_mix_pba <- mod_ml_pba$sample(
    data = mixed_stan_data, chains = 4, parallel_chains = 4,
    iter_warmup = 1000, iter_sampling = 1000,
    adapt_delta = 0.95, refresh = 0
  )

  fit_mix_wba <- mod_ml_wba$sample(
    data = mixed_stan_data, chains = 4, parallel_chains = 4,
    iter_warmup = 1000, iter_sampling = 1000,
    adapt_delta = 0.95, refresh = 0
  )

  # Save each fit individually with $save_object() so draws survive sessions
  fit_mix_sba$save_object("simmodels/ch10_fit_mix_sba.rds")
  fit_mix_pba$save_object("simmodels/ch10_fit_mix_pba.rds")
  fit_mix_wba$save_object("simmodels/ch10_fit_mix_wba.rds")

} else {
  fit_mix_sba <- readRDS("simmodels/ch10_fit_mix_sba.rds")
  fit_mix_pba <- readRDS("simmodels/ch10_fit_mix_pba.rds")
  fit_mix_wba <- readRDS("simmodels/ch10_fit_mix_wba.rds")
}

Code
# Decompose into per-agent ELPD sums
agent_elpd <- tibble(
  trial        = seq_len(mixed_stan_data$N),
  agent_id     = mixed_stan_data$agent_id,
  elpd_sba     = loo_mix_sba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"],
  elpd_pba     = loo_mix_pba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"],
  elpd_wba     = loo_mix_wba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"]
) |>
  group_by(agent_id) |>
  summarise(
    elpd_SBA = sum(elpd_sba),
    elpd_PBA = sum(elpd_pba),
    elpd_WBA = sum(elpd_wba),
    .groups  = "drop"
  ) |>
  left_join(mixed_truth |> dplyr::select(agent_id, true_strategy), by = "agent_id") |>
  mutate(
    best_model = case_when(
      elpd_SBA >= elpd_PBA & elpd_SBA >= elpd_WBA ~ "SBA",
      elpd_PBA >= elpd_SBA & elpd_PBA >= elpd_WBA ~ "PBA",
      TRUE ~ "WBA"
    ),
    # ELPD advantage of the best flexible model over the SBA baseline
    elpd_advantage = pmax(elpd_PBA, elpd_WBA) - elpd_SBA
  )




Code
# Left: per-agent ELPD advantage coloured by true strategy
p_agent_advantage <- ggplot(agent_elpd,
       aes(x = reorder(agent_id, elpd_advantage),

  )

# Right: scatter of per-agent SBA vs WBA ELPD

p_agent_advantage | p_agent_scatter









The bar chart on the left reveals a clean separation. The WBA agents (purple) consistently show positive ELPD advantages — the flexible model predicts their behaviour much better than the SBA. The SBA agents (green) hover near zero or show slight negative advantages — the SBA is sufficient, and the flexible models’ extra parameters represent wasted complexity. The scatter plot on the right makes the same point geometrically: WBA agents sit above the diagonal (ML-WBA predicts better), while SBA agents sit on or below it.

12.26.7 Visualising the Classification Boundary
What separates correctly classified agents from misclassified ones? We plot the agents in the estimated \((\rho, \kappa)\) space, marking classifications.


Code
mix_class_data <- mix_recovery |>
  left_join(agent_elpd |> dplyr::select(agent_id, best_model, elpd_advantage),
            by = "agent_id") |>
  mutate(correct = (true_strategy == "SBA" & best_model == "SBA") |
                   (true_strategy == "WBA" & best_model %in% c("PBA", "WBA")))

ggplot(mix_class_data,
       aes(x = est_rho, y = est_kappa,
           colour = true_strategy, shape = correct)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 2.0, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_point(size = 3.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("TRUE" = 16, "FALSE" = 4),
                     labels = c("TRUE" = "Correct", "FALSE" = "Misclassified")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Classification in Estimated Parameter Space",
    subtitle = "Dotted lines show SBA reference values (ρ = 0.5, κ = 2.0). Crosses = misclassified.",
    x        = expression("Estimated " * rho[j]),
    y        = expression("Estimated " * kappa[j]),
    colour   = "True Strategy", shape = "Classification"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")









Misclassifications, when they occur, concentrate near the boundary between the two strategy clusters — agents whose estimated \(\rho\) sits between 0.35 and 0.5, where the SBA and WBA strategies are hardest to discriminate.


12.26.8 Discussion: What the Mixed-Population Analysis Reveals
This analysis demonstrates three important lessons for applied cognitive modelling:
1. Population parameters are misleading for heterogeneous groups. The ML-WBA’s estimated population mean \(\rho\) averaged over two qualitatively different strategies to produce a summary that describes nobody. This is not a failure of the hierarchical model per se — it is a structural consequence of fitting a unimodal population distribution to a bimodal mixture. When you suspect strategy heterogeneity, do not interpret population means at face value.
2. Individual-level recovery succeeds despite population-level misspecification. The ML-WBA, though structurally unable to represent a mixture population, still recovers individual \(\rho_j\) and \(\kappa_j\) values well enough to separate the two groups. The estimated population SD absorbs the between-group variance, and the partial pooling is mild enough that the bimodal structure in the individual estimates is preserved. A formal mixture model (as in Ch. 9) would be more appropriate — it would estimate the mixing proportion and the within-group population distributions directly — but the standard hierarchical model is a surprisingly robust approximation.
3. Per-agent model comparison identifies strategy types. By decomposing the overall LOO into agent-level contributions, we can classify individuals as SBA-type or WBA-type with reasonable accuracy. This connects directly to the mixture modelling approach from Ch. 9: where a mixture model infers strategy membership within the generative model, per-agent LOO achieves a similar classification post hoc through predictive comparison.
The practical implication for researchers: if you suspect your participants use different strategies, always look beyond population-level summaries. Examine individual parameter estimates for multimodality, and use per-agent model comparison to classify participants before interpreting group effects.

title: 12  Bayesian Models of Cognition
text:
12.27 Beyond Weighted Bayes: Circular Inference
The models we have explored so far — SBA, PBA, WBA — share a common structural assumption: each source of evidence is counted exactly once. Jardri and Denève (2013) showed that this assumption can be systematically violated when information reverberates through a recurrent network: evidence feeds forward, is partially reflected back, and is counted again. They called this circular inference. Simonsen et al. (2021) provided behavioural evidence that such overcounting occurs in patients with schizophrenia, and derived a family of log-odds models — ranging from simple face-value integration, through weighted integration, to full circular inference with and without interference — that quantify the degree of reverberation.
Chapter 12 (Circular Inference in Social Learning) develops this model family in full. It grounds the Jardri models in the Simonsen et al. empirical dataset, adapts the pseudo-count agents from this chapter to the actual experimental design, implements and validates the four-model log-odds family with Simulation-Based Calibration Checks, selects the best model via LOO comparison, and interprets its parameters as group-level markers distinguishing healthy controls from patients with schizophrenia.
The key mathematical machinery — the Jardri transformation function \(F(L,w)\) and its three-stage extension to loops and interference — is introduced and illustrated there. The present chapter’s SBA, PBA, and WBA provide the natural comparison baseline.

12.27.1 The Log-Odds Transformation Function
The core mathematical object in the Jardri family is a non-linear weighting function defined in log-odds space. If \(L\) is the log-odds of a piece of evidence and \(w \in [0.5, 1]\) is a trust weight, then:
\[F(L,w) = \log \frac{w e^L + 1 - w}{(1 - w)e^L + w}\]
At \(w = 1\), \(F(L, 1) = L\) (evidence taken at face value). At \(w = 0.5\), \(F(L, 0.5) = 0\) (evidence completely discarded). The Jardri family then proceeds in three stages: Weighted Bayes (weights only), Circular Inference without interference (weights + loop multipliers \(\alpha \geq 1\)), and full Circular Inference with interference. Chapter 13 implements all four members of this family, validates them with Simulation-Based Calibration Checks, and applies them to the Simonsen et al. dataset.


12.27.2 The Model Family in Brief
The four models in the log-odds family differ only in how many structural additions they allow:








Model
Structural addition
Parameters




Simple Bayes
None (face-value integration)
0


Weighted Bayes
Differential trust per source
\(w_s, w_o \in [0.5, 1]\)


CI without interference
Per-source loop multipliers
\(+\,\alpha_s, \alpha_o \geq 1\)


CI with interference
Reverberated signal corrupts base evidence
same as above



Chapter 13 provides the Stan code, Simulation-Based Calibration Checks, and empirical fits for each member of this family.

title: 12  Bayesian Models of Cognition
text:
12.28 What the Bayesian Workflow Adds
This chapter has done more than fit cognitive models — it has validated them through a disciplined pipeline. Prior predictive checks established that the priors of all three models are cognitively plausible. SBC certified that the Stan samplers for the WBA and PBA are unbiased. Single-fit recovery confirmed identifiability. LOO-PIT and sensitivity analysis characterised the limits of the fitted posteriors. The three-way PSIS-LOO comparison arbitrated between competing cognitive hypotheses in a way that penalises complexity and estimates genuine predictive performance.
This pipeline is not bureaucracy. Without SBC, a subtle Stan implementation bug could produce attractive posteriors that are systematically biased. Without LOO-PIT, we cannot distinguish a model that fits from a model that truly predicts. Without the three-way comparison, we cannot know whether the WBA’s additional parameter reflects a real cognitive structure or mere overfitting. And without parameter recovery, we would never have discovered that the raw \((w_d, w_s)\) parameterisation was structurally unidentifiable — a problem invisible to standard MCMC diagnostics.

12.28.1 Limitations of the Current Models

The observation model is itself a hypothesis. The BetaBinomial claims participants probability-match — sampling their action from the full posterior over \(\theta\). If participants are more deterministic (choosing blue whenever \(\text{E}[\theta] > 0.5\)), the BetaBinomial will overestimate response variability. A softmax observation model with an inverse-temperature parameter would test this alternative; the LOO-PIT hump pattern is the diagnostic signature to watch for.
Trial independence in non-sequential models. The SBA, PBA, and WBA assume each trial involves a fresh, independent jar. Applying these models to paradigms with across-trial structure requires the sequential extension — with LFO-CV replacing standard LOO.
Fixed prior pseudo-count. We fix \(\alpha_0 = \beta_0 = 0.5\) (Jeffreys prior), assuming no prior knowledge about the jar. A hierarchical extension would estimate a population distribution over \((\alpha_0, \beta_0)\), allowing for individual differences in baseline expectations.
The PBA’s identifiability boundary. When \(p \to 0\) or \(p \to 1\), one evidence source is entirely ignored. The Beta(2,2) prior keeps the sampler away from these boundaries, but check tail ESS whenever the PBA is fitted to strongly asymmetric agents.
Social evidence encoding and pool-size asymmetry. Social evidence is encoded as total2 = 3 pseudo-marbles, making direct and social evidence structurally asymmetric. Recovered weights should be interpreted relative to the encoding, not as absolute measures of social reliance.



12.28.2 Connections and Extensions

Rational inattention models relax the assumption that all evidence is perfectly observed — the agent may allocate limited attentional resources to each source.
Resource-rational models (Griffiths et al. 2015) ask: what integration strategy is optimal given limited computational resources? The WBA can be interpreted as an approximation to full Bayesian inference under resource constraints.
Active inference (Friston 2010) frames perception and action as a unified process of minimising variational free energy, offering a different theoretical foundation for the same empirical phenomena.







NoteStan Engineering: What We Have Built Across Chapters 4–10



Across this book’s first ten chapters we have introduced a layered set of Stan programming techniques. Before moving to the categorization chapters — where all of them combine — it is worth having the full picture in one place.
Stage I (Ch. 4–5): Foundations







Technique
Rule




Block placement
transformed data for data-only quantities; transformed parameters for parameter-dependent state; model for local accumulators; generated quantities for post-sampling diagnostics


run_diagnostics flag
Gate expensive generated quantities (PPC draws, log_lik) behind an int<lower=0,upper=1> data flag; set to 0 in SBC backends


Priors as data
Pass prior hyperparameters through the data block — no recompilation when adjusting priors; SBC stays in sync automatically


Pathfinder warm-start
Run mod$pathfinder(...) first; pass the result as init to mod$sample(...) to cut warm-up by 30–60% on complex models



Stage II (Ch. 6): Population Structure







Technique
Rule




Non-centered parameterization (NCP)
Sample standardized offsets \(z_j \sim N(0,1)\); reconstruct cognitive parameters deterministically in transformed parameters. Eliminates Neal’s Funnel in sparse-data hierarchical models


Subject indexing
subj_id array (scatter-index) for exchangeable models; subj_start/subj_end contiguous-block for sequential models (Ch. 9+)



Stage III (Ch. 8–10): Process Mechanisms







Technique
Rule




Discrete marginalization
Never sample discrete parameters with HMC; use log_sum_exp or log_mix to analytically integrate them out in the model block


Sequential state unrolling
Recursive belief/memory state goes in transformed parameters with volatile state kept local in {}; only the quantities needed downstream are lifted to the saved-draw tape



Stage IV (Ch. 11): High-Performance
The categorization chapters introduce the final layer: precomputing pairwise stimulus distances in transformed data (computed once rather than thousands of times), NCP for all individual parameters including attention weights (eliminating Dirichlet funnels), and reduce_sum for multi-threaded parallelism across subjects within a chain. The next chapter puts all of these together.


This is the end of the core modelling sequence. Chapters 1–10 have traced the complete Bayesian cognitive modelling workflow: from pizza-stone physics (Ch. 1), through verbal and formal models of strategic behaviour (Chs. 2–3), parameter estimation (Ch. 4), model validation (Ch. 5), hierarchical extensions (Ch. 6), model comparison (Ch. 7), mixture structures (Ch. 8), sequential cognition (Ch. 9), and Bayesian cognitive theory (Ch. 10). The tools and habits of mind established here — generative modelling, principled validation, honest uncertainty — apply directly to any new domain or model you encounter.

title: 12  Bayesian Models of Cognition
text:

title: 13  Circular Inference in Social Learning
text:
13.1 Learning Objectives
After completing this chapter, you will be able to:
crumbs: 13  Circular Inference in Social Learning

title: 13  Circular Inference in Social Learning
text:
Describe the circular inference hypothesis and the experimental paradigm used to test it.
Translate continuous-probability evidence (as recorded in a real experiment) into the count format required by pseudo-count Bayesian agents.
Adapt Simple, Proportional, and Weighted Bayesian Agent models to a new experimental design and fit them to empirical data.
Implement the four-model log-odds family (Simple Bayes, Weighted Bayes, Circular Inference without and with Interference) in Stan and validate them via Simulation-Based Calibration Checks.
Select the best-fitting model via LOO comparison, assess its quality with posterior predictive checks, and interpret its coefficients and marginal effects.
Compare model parameters across clinical groups to draw substantive conclusions about the cognitive mechanisms underlying schizophrenia.
crumbs: 13  Circular Inference in Social Learning

title: 13  Circular Inference in Social Learning
text:
13.2 Introduction: Circular Inference and Schizophrenia
Chapter 11 ended with a puzzle. The weighted Bayesian agents (SBA, PBA, WBA) all share one structural assumption: each source of evidence is counted exactly once. Yet there is a specific class of phenomena — confident delusions, ideas of reference, the tendency to “jump to conclusions” — that require evidence to be amplified, not merely weighted. Jardri and Denève (2013) proposed that such amplification arises when information reverberates in a recurrent neural network: evidence feeds forward, gets partially reflected back, and is counted again. They called this circular inference.
Simonsen et al. (2021) formalised this hypothesis in a behavioural paradigm (the marble task) and tested it in a sample of patients with schizophrenia and matched healthy controls. Their key finding was that patients showed elevated loop parameters — they overcounted their own direct evidence — and reduced social weighting, consistent with the phenomenology of self-referential thinking and social withdrawal in schizophrenia.
This chapter replicates and extends that analysis using the framework we built in Chapter 11. We proceed through four stages:

Data exploration — understanding the structure of the empirical dataset and how participants actually integrate self and social evidence.
Pseudo-count models — adapting SBA, PBA, and WBA to the specific experimental design and fitting them to the data.
Log-odds models — implementing and validating the full Jardri family (Simple Bayes, Weighted Bayes, Circular Inference without and with Interference).
Model comparison and interpretation — selecting the best model, checking its fit, and comparing its parameters between groups.
crumbs: 13  Circular Inference in Social Learning

title: 13  Circular Inference in Social Learning
text:
13.3 The Empirical Dataset

13.3.1 Study Design
The study used a social perceptual decision-making task (the “red/blue marble” task). On each trial:

A participant drew 8 marbles from an urn. The proportion of red marbles provides their self evidence: \(p_\text{self} = k_\text{self} / 8\).
Four other participants made their own draws from the same urn and reported whether they thought the next marble would be red or blue (binary responses). The proportion of those four responding “red” is the social evidence: \(p_\text{social} \in \{0, 1/4, 1/2, 3/4, 1\}\).
The participant then chose: red (1) or blue/green (0).

The design crossed seven self-evidence levels (\(p_\text{self} \in \{1/8, \ldots, 7/8\}\)) with five social-evidence levels, giving 35 unique evidence combinations. Each participant completed 105–210 trials.
Evidence encoding for models. Both pseudo-count and log-odds models require evidence expressed as counts. For self evidence the count is direct: \(k_\text{self} = \text{round}(p_\text{self} \times 8) \in \{1,\ldots,7\}\), \(n_\text{self} = 8\). The four binary social responses give a raw count of \(k^\star_\text{social} = \text{round}(p_\text{social} \times 4) \in \{0,1,2,3,4\}\), \(n^\star_\text{social} = 4\). To put both sources on the same scale — so that a unanimous social cue contributes the same maximum pseudo-counts as a unanimous self cue — we rescale to an equivalent 8-vote sample: \(k_\text{social} = 2 \, k^\star_\text{social}\), \(n_\text{social} = 8\). This is purely a modeling choice that eliminates a structural self/social asymmetry in the SBA. In the log-odds family the rescaling also affects the Jeffreys-smoothing term, but the impact on predicted probabilities is small.
Two groups were recruited: - Controls (Group = 0): healthy adults. - Patients (Group = 1): individuals diagnosed with schizophrenia.


13.3.2 Data Loading and Preparation


Code
df_raw <- read_csv(here("data", "CleanCircularDatav2.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

df <- df_raw |>
  mutate(
    choice_red   = as.integer(RedChoice),
    # Self evidence: 1–7 red marbles out of 8
    k_self       = as.integer(round(Self * 8)),
    n_self       = 8L,
    # Social evidence: rescale 0–4 out-of-4 to 0–8 out-of-8 for equal scale
    k_social     = as.integer(round(Others * 8)),  # 0,2,4,6,8
    n_social     = 8L,
    # Jeffreys-smoothed log-odds (computed after rescaling)
    lo_self      = log((0.5 + k_self)   / (0.5 + n_self   - k_self)),
    lo_social    = log((0.5 + k_social) / (0.5 + n_social - k_social)),
    group_label  = if_else(Group == 0, "Control", "Schizophrenia"),
    participant  = paste0(SubjectPair, "_", Group)
  )

cat("Rows:", nrow(df), "\n")


Rows: 7797 


Code
cat("Unique participants:", n_distinct(df$participant), "\n")


Unique participants: 75 


Code
df |> count(group_label) |> print()


# A tibble: 2 × 2
  group_label       n
  <chr>         <int>
1 Control        4200
2 Schizophrenia  3597




13.3.3 Data Exploration

13.3.3.1 Participant Overview


Code
trials_per_pp <- df |>
  count(participant, group_label, name = "n_trials")

ggplot(trials_per_pp, aes(x = n_trials, fill = group_label)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10, colour = "white", alpha = 0.8,
                 position = "identity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_group) +
  labs(
    title = "Trials per Participant",
    x = "Number of trials", y = "Count", fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")











13.3.3.2 Evidence Distributions


Code
p_self_dist <- df |>
  distinct(participant, k_self, group_label) |>
  count(k_self, group_label) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(k_self), y = n, fill = group_label)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_group) +
  labs(title = "Self Evidence", x = "Red marbles drawn (out of 8)",
       y = "Count", fill = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p_social_dist <- df |>
  count(k_social, group_label) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(k_social), y = n, fill = group_label)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_group) +
  labs(title = "Social Evidence (rescaled to 0–8)",
       x = "Social evidence k_social (out of 8)", y = "Count", fill = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

p_self_dist + p_social_dist











13.3.3.3 Psychometric Curves
The most informative descriptive analysis is the psychometric curve: how does the probability of choosing red change with self evidence, for each level of social evidence?


Code
psycho_df <- df |>
  mutate(social_label = paste0(k_social, " / 8")) |>
  group_by(group_label, k_self, social_label) |>
  summarise(
    p_red  = mean(choice_red),
    n      = n(),
    se     = sqrt(p_red * (1 - p_red) / n),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(psycho_df,
       aes(x = k_self, y = p_red,
           colour = social_label, group = social_label)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = p_red - se, ymax = p_red + se,
                  fill = social_label),
              alpha = 0.12, colour = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.4) +
  facet_wrap(~group_label) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal_social) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_social) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:7) +
  labs(
    title    = "Empirical Psychometric Curves",
    subtitle = "Proportion choosing red (± SE) by self evidence and social evidence",
    x        = "Red marbles drawn (self evidence)",
    y        = "P(choose red)",
    colour   = "Social\nevidence",
    fill     = "Social\nevidence"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")









Both groups show the expected main effects: more red self evidence increases P(choose red), and more social evidence for red shifts the curves upward. Patients appear to show steeper self-evidence curves (suggesting stronger self-weighting or overcounting) and somewhat compressed social-evidence separation (suggesting less social influence). We will quantify these impressions with models.


13.3.3.4 Overall Accuracy


Code
df |>
  group_by(group_label, participant) |>
  summarise(acc = mean(choice_red == as.integer(
    (k_self / n_self + k_social / n_social) / 2 > 0.5)),
    .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(group_label) |>
  summarise(mean_acc = mean(acc), sd_acc = sd(acc), .groups = "drop")


# A tibble: 2 × 3
  group_label   mean_acc sd_acc
  <chr>            <dbl>  <dbl>
1 Control          0.829 0.0616
2 Schizophrenia    0.763 0.0606
